q: The relations between linear and angular quantities were first studied in Grade 12.
In rotational motion, all particles of an object rotate around an axis of rotation but do not necessarily move in a circular path.
The arc length $s$ and angular displacement $\theta$ are related by $s = r \theta$, where $\theta$ must be measured in radians.
The instantaneous angular velocity $\omega$ is unique for each particle within a rotating object.
The magnitude of linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ are related by $v = r \omega$, where $\omega$ is measured in radians per second.
Linear velocity is also known as tangential velocity because its direction is tangent to the path.
A change in the magnitude of a rotating object's angular velocity results in tangential acceleration.
The direction of tangential acceleration is always radial, towards the center of the circular path.
Tangential acceleration $a_t$ is related to angular acceleration $\alpha$ by $a_t = r \alpha$.
Tangential acceleration measures how fast the linear velocity changes.
The angular velocity vector's direction is parallel to the plane of rotation.
The angular velocity vector points along the axis of rotation.
The right-hand rule is used to determine the direction of linear velocity.
According to the right-hand rule, if fingers point in the direction of rotation, the thumb points in the direction of the angular velocity vector.
If angular velocity is increasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the direction opposite to the angular velocity.
If angular velocity is decreasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the same direction as the angular velocity.
Linear quantities are denoted by $s$, $v$, and $a$.
Angular quantities are denoted by $\theta$, $\omega$, and $\alpha$.
The relation $v = r \omega$ applies to the entire rotational object, not just individual particles.
Angular displacement is measured in degrees when using the relation $s = r \theta$.
Tangential acceleration is a measure of how fast the tangential velocity changes.
The angular velocity vector is perpendicular to the plane of rotation.
The right-hand rule is used to determine the magnitude of angular velocity.
When the angular velocity is constant, there is no tangential acceleration.
The direction of linear velocity is always perpendicular to the radius for an object in circular motion.
The angular velocity of a rotational object can vary for different particles within the same object.
The relation $a_t = r \alpha$ connects tangential acceleration, radius, and angular displacement.
The magnitude of linear velocity will only change if the radius of the circular path changes.
The linear distance measured along an arc is denoted by $\theta$.
The angular displacement is denoted by $s$.
The linear velocity is always directed along the radius of the circular path.
The angular acceleration vector's direction is independent of whether angular velocity is increasing or decreasing.
The relation $s=r\theta$ is applicable even if $\theta$ is expressed in degrees.
The tangential acceleration vector is always perpendicular to the direction of the circular path.
The relations between linear and angular quantities are identical for both circular and rotational motion.
The relations between linear and angular quantities (s, v, a and $\theta$, $\omega$, $\alpha$) are introduced for the first time in Grade 12.
The linear velocity $v$ is sometimes referred to as tangential speed.
The angular acceleration $\alpha$ is measured in radians per second cubed.
If the magnitude of angular velocity is constant, then the tangential acceleration is non-zero.
A particle of a rotating object moves in a circular path that is parallel to the axis of rotation.
